Geographic Audit  ·  Primary Source Evidence  ·  USGS 1894

The Terrain Verdict

Why Edendale Could Not Become Hollywood — 1894 Federal Survey & Modern Topographic Evidence

Primary Source

The 1894 USGS Los Angeles Quadrangle

U.S. Geological Survey · Los Angeles Sheet · Scale 1:62,500 · Filed May 19, 1897

The argument does not require estimation. It is written on a federal topographic survey completed thirteen years before Boggs arrived and nineteen years before DeMille signed his barn lease. The terrain preceded the industry. The industry had no choice but to respond to it.

Figure 1 · Primary Source · Federal Survey · 1894
Hollywood Plain & Edendale Canyon — The Full Contrast, One Frame
USGS Los Angeles Quad, 1894 · Scale ~1:36,000
Contour interval: 25 ft · Spot elevations in feet
ngmdb.usgs.gov/topoview · HTMC 1894 ed.
Hollywood · 356 ft · flat grid Edendale canyon · 500–903 ft Both zones · same federal survey · same scale · 1894
Read directly from this image: Left side — the Hollywood/Colegrove grid with spot elevation 356 ft labeled, zero contour lines across every block. Right side — the Edendale canyon system: the 500 ft contour labeled multiple times, 600 ft labeled, 734 ft labeled mid-canyon, dense contour bands packing toward Elysian Park. The cursor (blue dot) sits at the canyon edge — the exact transition point from bajada plain to canyon system. This contrast, in one frame, on a 1894 federal survey, is the entire geographic argument for why Hollywood absorbed the film industry and Edendale could not.
Figure 2 · Detail · Hollywood Close-up
Hollywood Grid — 356 ft Spot Elevation in Detail
USGS Los Angeles Quad, 1894
Hollywood / Colegrove / Prospect Park
500 ft contour at canyon fringe only
The 356 ft reading in detail: A USGS field surveyor measured this point and labeled it on the federal document. Zero contour lines cross the Hollywood/Colegrove grid — with a 25-ft contour interval, cross-slope is below the survey's detection threshold. The 500 ft contour appears only at the canyon fringe upper-right. This is the flatness that made Lasky's 9-acre expansion in 1914 physically possible.
Figure 3 · Detail · Canyon Close-up
Canyon System — Contour Density vs. Hollywood Blank Grid
USGS Los Angeles Quad, 1894
Los Felis canyon · Ivanhoe · Edgemont
825 ft and 903 ft ridge spot elevations
Contour density contrast: Hollywood grid (lower-left) — blank. Canyon system (upper-left, Los Felis) — contour lines packed so dense they form near-solid brown bands. Ridge spot elevations: 825 ft and 903 ft labeled. The 500 ft contour runs through the canyon corridor that becomes Allesandro Street — the future studio row. Eight to twelve contour lines cross a distance where the Hollywood side has zero.
The Numbers

All Values Read from the 1894 Federal Survey

25-ft contour interval · labeled spot elevations · no interpolation required

Edendale · Canyon Floor
~500 ft
500 ft contour · Ivanhoe/Edgemont corridor
Labeled contour through the canyon that becomes Allesandro Street — the future studio row.
Edendale · Canyon Ridge
903 ft
labeled spot elevation · canyon wall · 1894
825 ft and 903 ft labeled on the flanking ridges. The walls that made lateral expansion physically impossible.
Edendale · Total Relief
~400 ft
floor to ridge · ~1 mile horizontal
~400 ft of vertical relief within one mile. No studio lot here could expand without blasting into hillside.
Hollywood · Studio Plain
356 ft
spot elevation · Hollywood/Colegrove · 1894
Labeled directly inside the Hollywood grid on the federal survey. Zero contour lines in studio blocks. A field measurement, not an estimate.
Hollywood · Cross-slope
<25 ft
zero contour lines · 25-ft interval threshold
No contour lines cross the studio blocks. Cross-slope is below the survey's detection threshold.
The Gap · Same Map
547 ft
903 ft ridge minus 356 ft Hollywood plain
547 ft difference between canyon ridge and studio plain — both labeled on the same 1894 page. Not an estimate. A reading.

~500 ft floor → 903 ft ridge

356 ft · <25 ft cross-slope
Spot elevation inside Hollywood/Colegrove grid · zero contour lines in studio blocks

Edendale vs. Hollywood — c. 1894–1913

FeatureEdendale — The TrapHollywood — The Plain
Geological formationCanyon floor / hillsideAlluvial fan (Bajada)

Plain-to-ridge gap547 ft (903 ft ridge minus 356 ft Hollywood plain — both labeled, same 1894 map)
Canyon relief~400 ft floor-to-ridge
~1 mile horiz. · pre-freeway · steeper than modern data		<25 ft cross-slope
Below 25-ft contour threshold across studio blocks
Modern corroboration~8% grade (680→801 ft, ~1,500 ft horiz.)
Post-1959 freeway grading — original steeper		<2% · ~400 ft modern reading
Consistent with 1894 356 ft value
Studio acreage ceiling~0.75–1 acre
Canyon walls on 3 sides · impossible to expand		Unlimited flat land
Lasky: 9 ac by 1914. Fox: 15 ac. Paramount: dozens.
Lot geometryRibbon lots · 50–100 ft wide
Runs into hillside · linear only		Grid parcels · square · combinable
Former ranch land · buildable in any direction
Stage constructionImpossible at feature scale
~400 ft canyon relief = excavation cost exceeds land value		No structural constraint
<25 ft cross-slope = standard foundation
Format servedOne-reel (10–15 min)
Chase comedies, canyon scenics — terrain shaped the format		Feature-length (60+ min)
Standing sets, backlots — requires flat industrial acreage
The Math

The Stage Construction Problem

The shift to features around 1912–13 made one piece of infrastructure essential: the glass-roofed stage. A large, level, skylit interior was not an amenity — it was a technical requirement. The 1894 map makes the construction problem immediately legible.

Edendale · The Trap · 1894 USGS
Canyon Ribbon
Glendale Blvd / Allesandro corridor
Canyon relief — 1894 primary source
~400 ft
floor-to-ridge · labeled USGS values · pre-freeway
A 100-ft glass stage on this canyon requires retaining walls or excavating thousands of tons of hillside. Cost exceeds land value. The stage cannot exist here.
Maximum studio acreage
~1 ac
Selig lot · canyon walls on 3 sides
William Fox, same lot: 0.75 acres. The canyon that made Edendale cinematic made it industrially terminal.
Lot geometry
Ribbon
50–100 ft wide · runs into hillside
A backlot "New York Street" beside "Paris Street" would have a 30-ft grade change between them. Not a design problem — a physics problem.
Hollywood · The Plain · 1894 USGS
Bajada Floor
Sunset–Gower / Selma–Vine corridor
Plain elevation — 1894 primary source
356 ft
spot elevation · Hollywood/Colegrove · <25 ft cross-slope
A 100-ft glass stage on <25 ft cross-slope = standard foundation pour. No excavation. No retaining walls. Flat former citrus grove.
Expansion acreage achieved
9 ac
Lasky Feature Play Co. · 1914 · from same barn
Rented barn, Dec 1913. Flat citrus groves on all sides meant Lasky could simply buy the block. Nine times Edendale's ceiling in one year.
Lot geometry
Grid
ranch parcels · square · combinable
Flat former agricultural land. Buildable in any direction at grade. The industrial floor that feature filmmaking required.
Modern Evidence

MapCortex Topographic Layer, 2026

130 years later — after the 1959 Glendale Freeway graded away portions of the original canyon — the same contrast holds. Modern data understates the original case. The canyon measurable today is shallower than the one Boggs and Sennett filmed in.

Edendale — Canyon Terrain
Modern layer: elevation labels 680–801 ft every 2–3 blocks. Dense contours. Post-1959 freeway grading — original terrain was steeper.
Hollywood — Bajada Plain
Modern layer: zero elevation labels across entire studio district at equivalent zoom. Same bajada plain shown in 1894, unchanged.

MapCortex topographic layer · USGS / OpenStreetMap · March 2026 · equivalent zoom levels.

Filmmakers didn't discover Hollywood. They were evicted by the geometry of Edendale. The canyon gave early cinema its visual language — chase, cliff, pursuit. Then it gave the industry a ceiling it could not break through. Hollywood offered the only thing Edendale couldn't: the horizontal.

The Terrain Verdict  ·  Geographic Audit, c. 1913
Primary source: USGS Los Angeles Quadrangle, 1894 (HTMC edition), Scale 1:62,500, filed May 19, 1897. Available at ngmdb.usgs.gov/topoview. Contour interval: 25 ft. All cited values — 356 ft (Hollywood/Colegrove grid spot elevation), ~500 ft (canyon floor contour, Ivanhoe/Edgemont corridor), 600 ft and 734 ft (mid-canyon labeled contours/elevations), 825 ft and 903 ft (ridge spot elevations) — labeled directly on the federal survey. No interpolation. Secondary: MapCortex topographic layer (USGS/OpenStreetMap), March 2026. Modern 680→801 ft Edendale reading is post-1959 Glendale Freeway grading — original canyon was steeper. Hollywood ~400 ft modern reading consistent with 1894 356 ft value. Municipal: Silver Lake–Echo Park–Elysian Valley Community Plan (City of Los Angeles, 2004): "fairly dense, hillside neighborhoods characterized by steep slopes and narrow streets."
